role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 6.6% + 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% in the 4-digit 5418 proxy — real media-buying firms are dominated by planner/buyer/investment staff largely spread across these SOCs and the tail; est. ~30-40% of actual wage bill
role_hints.note: Plan building, audience research, RFPs to vendors, flighting, pacing, optimization and reporting are heavily AI-exposed — platforms automate much of this natively; vendor negotiation, principal deal-making and client strategy persist. Plausible within-role fraction 0.4-0.6.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; ad-ops/billing-reconciliation staff in the tail, est. ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Trafficking, tag management, make-goods, discrepancy reconciliation and media billing are the most directly automatable cost lines in the business.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2% (proxy)
role_hints.note: Client relationships and media-vendor relationships (rate leverage) persist; internal management automates. Only 663 firms in the code — many are founder-led.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; analysts/data engineers in the tail, est. ~6-10%
role_hints.note: Attribution, MMM-lite reporting and dashboarding automate heavily; measurement design judgment persists at the margin.
Status reporting and plan presentations automate; relationship ownership persists.

role_hints.role: Back office and remaining tail (incl. proxy contaminants)
role_hints.oews_basis: 4-digit top-10 covers ~51% of wage bill and includes non-media-buying occupations (27-3031 PR Specialists 8.3%, 27-1024 Graphic Designers 3.3%, 41-9011 Demonstrators 1.6%, 27-1026 Merchandise Displayers 1.2%) — treat as contamination; true remainder is finance/admin
role_hints.note: Finance/billing admin in a business that reconciles high-volume media invoices — strongly automatable.

source_hints.source: COMvergence media agency billings, pitch and new-business tracking
source_hints.why: The standard tracker of media-buying account movements and billings — market structure and client-stickiness evidence.

source_hints.source: ANA media transparency studies (2016 K2 report and successor programmatic-transparency work)
source_hints.why: Primary documentation of principal-based buying, rebates and undisclosed margin — the capture mechanism (and its client-side backlash) in this industry.
Quantifies how much media buying is already automated/programmatic — the baseline for A.
Deal flow and multiples for media-services transactions.

source_hints.source: Madison and Wall (Brian Wieser) advertising market analyses
source_hints.why: Independent analyst coverage of agency economics and AI's effect on media-buying revenue models.

capture_questions: Media buying is priced as a % of media spend (commission), fixed fees benchmarked to spend, or undisclosed principal-trading margin — none of which are hour-based, so the operator keeps AI savings as margin UNTIL clients or auditors reprice: does fee benchmarking (auditors like Ebiquity-type firms) and transparency pressure convert AI efficiency into commission compression — and how fast does the operator's keep erode?

Who owns the client relationship — the firm's media-vendor rate leverage and buying clout (scale-based, survives acquisition) or individual principals? Does a roll-up actually aggregate buying clout across acquired shops?

What stops SMB and mid-market clients from self-serving through Google Performance Max / Meta Advantage+ and retail media consoles — the exact client tier independent media shops serve — and what does client-count attrition data show?

Is principal-based buying margin available to small independents at all, or does it require holdco-scale inventory commitments — i.e., is the highest-capture pricing mechanism structurally out of reach for the band population?
terminal_value_question: The deciding mechanism is platform self-serve automation: Google, Meta, Amazon and retail media networks are building AI that plans, buys and optimizes natively, aimed exactly at the SMB/mid-market clients of the ~600 firms in this code. Commission pricing means the operator keeps AI's cost savings — but only on spend that still flows through them. Is the terminal state a smaller number of firms arbitraging cross-platform complexity and principal deals, or near-total disintermediation of the independent media buyer? Distinguish falling operating cost per dollar managed (margin story) from falling dollars managed (product replacement).
special_notes: Capture is unusually pricing-model-favorable (commission/% of spend, not hours) — the deflation risk arrives as commission-rate compression via fee auditors and transparency reviews, not as hour rescoping; model pi_dist through that channel and cite the ANA transparency lineage.

Revenue accounting trap (severe here): gross media billings vs net revenue — the record's labor_share of 0.2759 and revenue/employee of $435k suggest some firms' SUSB receipts include media pass-through; any revenue-based multiple or market-size figure must be normalized to net revenue/commission income.

Principal-based buying and rebates distort reported margins: some firms' EBITDA includes undisclosed media margin that transparency-era clients claw back — normalize and probe sustainability before applying multiples.
Small-N caution: n_total is 663 and n_band 121 — the roll-up universe is thin; a few large independents (Horizon-scale) dominate industry statistics and are not comps for band-sized targets.
Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy imports PR specialists, designers, demonstrators and display staff that do not exist in media-buying shops — use the adjusted structure and say so.

Boundary confusion: full-service agencies with media departments are 541810, media reps (selling side) are 541840, programmatic consultancies straddle 541830/541613/541511 — check what any survey or comp actually covers.
